How to Build Products That Actually LearnAs an engineer, my day-to-day is usually deep in the code. But lately, I’ve been fascinated with a different kind of architecture: the systems that bridge the gap between technical execution and business strategy to actually drive user growth.
What I’ve realized is that when a product struggles with engagement, what’s often missing isn’t “better features.” It’s that those features were never meant to talk to each other.
The deeper issue is that features are often built in isolation, with no orchestration between them. So when users arrive at the app, discoverability suffers, not because individual features are bad, but because they never form a cohesive system.
The real friction tends to live in the space between the features.
To solve this, we need to shift away from standard feature lists and roadmaps. We need to build a system where every experiment connects to the last one, feeds the next one, and makes the product smarter with every cycle.
I call this the Growth Loop Engine. Here is how it works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Growth Loop EngineFour Layers, One Repeating Cycle
The Growth Loop Engine is systems thinking in practice: every output becomes the input to the next cycle. The engine doesn’t just run. It learns. It consists of four distinct layers:
1. The Signal Layer
This layer surfaces the real problem. We don’t rely on whiteboard assumptions; we look for the actual friction a user is experiencing. It draws from customer data, competitive signals, and past experiment archives.
2. The Filter
This qualifies ideas before anyone touches a keyboard. It asks three simple questions:

• Does it solve a real user problem?

• Does it help the business?

• Can it be tested cheaply first?
3. The Gate
This is the checkpoint before building starts. We assign a success metric, confirm how the new feature connects to something else in the product, and crucially set a removal date upfront.
4. The Learning Loop
This is where speed becomes knowledge. Every experiment gets measured. Every decision (Scale, Iterate, or Kill) gets recorded. Nothing is wasted, not even the failures.
“A feature that gets killed but taught the team what drives engagement is infinitely more valuable than a feature that stays forever and teaches the team nothing.”
The 4 Lenses of Growth
Not every feature drives growth the same way. When designing an experiment, the question isn’t just “What should we build?” It’s “What kind of growth does this drive, and how will we know?”

• Amplifier: Organic reach without ad spend (e.g., users sharing a personalized summary on social media).

• Loyalty: Creates a reason to return (e.g., daily streaks or rewards).

• Trigger: Context-aware, not random (e.g., a personalized offer right when a user runs low on data).

• Community: Uses the social graph as a growth mechanism (e.g., a group pool for a shared reward).
Features that hit multiple lenses compound and create massive growth.
The Exit Ramp: The 30/60/90 Removal Rule
One of the hardest disciplines in product work is knowing when to let something go. Without a pre-agreed definition of what “not working” looks like, features tend to stay longer than they should.
To combat this, I use the 30/60/90 Rule:

• Day 30: Is anyone using it? (An early signal check, not a verdict, just a read).

• Day 60: Is it hitting the target? (Is it helping other features? This is where compound effects start to show).

• Day 90: Has it earned its place? (Based purely on data against a standard everyone already understood).
The Ultimate Payoff: Institutional Memory
When this engine runs, the conversation shifts from which features to build toward what the team is trying to learn.
Every experiment is documented. That archive becomes the team’s institutional memory. And as we look toward the future, this archive becomes the foundation for AI. By feeding this data into an AI model, it can read across every experiment the team has ever run, what worked, what didn’t, and why and automatically recognize trends that would take a human months to notice.
The goal isn’t to ship more. It’s to build the shortest path between a hypothesis and a decision, and to keep shortening it with every cycle.
A team that learns together builds better than a team that ships alone.
Signal → Filter → Gate → Learning Loop. The product doesn’t just ship. It compounds.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Ultimate Payoff: Institutional Memory
I’d love to hear from other developers, product managers, and growth leaders: What is the hardest part about getting a team to agree to kill a feature that isn’t working?
